title: Trap nesting to identify your best layers
subtitle: Using trap nests to record individual egg production and make informed breeding decisions.
excerpt: In a flock of 20 hens, the production range between the best and worst layer is typically 100 eggs or more per year. Trap nesting identifies which birds are pulling their weight and which are not. The data collected over four to six weeks is sufficient to make culling or breeding decisions that lift the whole flock's average performance within a generation.
sourceNotes: Lewis Wright, The Practical Poultry Keeper (1867), Internet Archive: trap nesting chapter on individual hen records. National Poultry Council, Poultry Husbandry (MAFF, 1940s), archive.org.

glossaryTerms.definition: A nest box fitted with a one-way entrance that allows a hen to enter but prevents her from leaving until released by the keeper. When the keeper checks and releases each trapped hen, they record which bird laid. The data builds an individual production record for each bird in the flock.

A per-bird tally of eggs collected over a defined period, typically maintained as a notebook or tally card with one row per hen. The record shows production frequency, any gaps (broodiness, moulting), and allows ranking of birds by output.

content.text: Most flock keepers collect eggs and have a rough sense of total output but no idea which hens are producing.
changes that. By trapping each hen as she exits the nest, the keeper builds a
that shows which birds are producing and which are eating feed without laying.

A standard rollaway nest box fitted with a one-way flap at the entrance functions as a basic trap nest. The hen pushes through the flap to enter and cannot push back out: the flap falls behind her. A small bolt or pin on the outside allows the keeper to close the entrance completely so only one hen at a time can be trapped per box.

You need one trap nest for every three to four hens in the flock. More boxes than this produces more reliable records; fewer means hens queue or are deterred from using the boxes during the busiest laying period (morning, roughly 8 am to noon).

Before beginning, every hen in the flock must be individually identifiable. Wing tags, numbered leg rings, or combinations of coloured cable ties on leg rings all work. A small management notebook with one row per bird is the simplest record format: date across the top, bird number down the side, an X or tally mark for each egg attributed to that bird.

A four-week recording period provides enough data to rank the flock confidently. Check and release trapped hens at least twice a day: mid-morning and mid-afternoon. A hen trapped for more than four hours is uncomfortable; trapped overnight is unacceptable.

Record the number and date each time you release a hen from a nest. After four weeks, total the count for each hen. The range between the highest and lowest producers is typically at least two to one, often three to one. The bottom third of the flock by production is the candidate pool for culling or replacement.

The simplest use of trap nest data is culling non-laying hens. A bird that laid fewer than five eggs in four weeks during the main laying season is either old, unwell, or genetically low-producing. Replacing that bird with a pullet from a high-producing line lifts the flock average immediately.

If you run a breeding programme, use the top 20% of birds by production as your hatching egg source. Set eggs from the best layers over several generations and bring in a cock from a high-producing line. The flock you build will outperform randomly bred birds of the same breed.

attrs.title: When trap nesting doesn't work well

content.text: Trap nesting during a moult, during a cold period that suppresses production, or in the weeks after birds have been moved or disturbed gives unreliable data. Run the trap-nesting period during a stable management phase when hens are in good lay. The records collected in autumn through winter will show lower numbers across the board; the relative ranking between birds is still valid, but the absolute counts are not comparable to spring production.
